description: The device exposes up to 32 general‑purpose I/O signals that can be used as inputs or outputs. Each pin can be independently configured and read back through dedicated registers. The GPIO module integrates direction control, data latches, edge detection, and interrupt generation. Banked summary and individual interrupt paths are supported for flexible event handling. All register addresses are defined by the device‑specific data manual.
evidence: SPRUF95 §2 Peripheral Architecture; Fig. 1; SPRS371 (base addresses)
Remarks: Use atomic set/clear paths for outputs to avoid read‑modify‑write hazards; confirm pinmux before enabling outputs.

description: A direction register controls whether each GPIO is an input or output. Software writes to the direction bits to place selected pins in output mode and leaves others as inputs. The current configuration can be read back to verify direction. Direction must be set before driving outputs to avoid contention. The register covers banks 0 and 1 for GP[31:0].
Remarks: Initialize DIR01 only after clocks are enabled; retain inputs at reset until mux and pull states are verified.

description: A per‑bank enable register gates whether GPIO bank summary interrupts are forwarded. Enabling the corresponding bank bit allows aggregated events to signal the CPU or router. It works with per‑pin edge enables and status to provide coarse filtering. Software should only enable banks after clearing stale status. The register is part of the interrupt control path for GPIO.
Remarks: Enable bank interrupts late in initialization to prevent spurious entries; pair with ISR that drains all pending bits.

description: The module provides readable input data and output latch registers. Inputs reflect the synchronized state of external pins and can be polled by software. The output latch reflects the last driven value for outputs. Reading these registers assists in debugging and in closed‑loop control. Accesses must follow clock and reset sequencing.
Remarks: Use input reads to clear metastability concerns through the internal synchronizers; avoid sampling before clocks stabilize.

description: Dedicated write‑one‑to‑set and write‑one‑to‑clear registers provide atomic changes to output bits. They avoid race conditions associated with read‑modify‑write on a shared OUTDATA register. These paths ensure glitch‑free updates when multiple bits must change concurrently. They are essential when GPIOs also generate interrupts or are observed by external logic. Software should prefer these registers for all output transitions.
Remarks: Batch updates using SET_DATA then CLR_DATA to form precise edges; do not RMW OUTDATA in interrupt contexts.

description: Each GPIO can be configured to detect rising and/or falling edges. Separate enable registers control rising and falling paths, and corresponding clear registers manage edge latches. Edge detection feeds into per‑bit status and bank summary logic. Correct configuration enables precise event capture without polling. Software must clear latched edges after servicing.
dependencies: BINTEN (if using bank summary); ISR installed
Remarks: Select a single edge when possible to reduce interrupt rate; clear latches promptly to avoid lost events.

description: A latched status register records which GPIOs have detected configured edges. Reading the register identifies active sources for service. Software is responsible for clearing the status to deassert requests. This mechanism allows level‑independent servicing of multiple pins. It integrates with both individual and banked interrupt paths.
Remarks: Always read and clear INSTAT within the ISR before re‑enabling interrupts to prevent retriggering.

description: A bank‑level status register summarizes pending GPIO events for banks 0 and 1. It allows quick identification of which bank and bit caused the interrupt. Software uses this register to dispatch to per‑pin handlers. Clearing underlying per‑bit status will clear the bank summary. It is a key part of scalable GPIO servicing.
Remarks: Use INTSTAT01 first in ISR to get a compact bitmap, then service and clear per‑bit latches.

feature_name: Individual GPIO interrupts to CPU (IFR path)
description: GPIO can signal individual interrupts that appear in the CPU’s Interrupt Flag Register. This path allows low‑latency service for critical pins without bank aggregation. CPU‑level enable and vector setup are required to receive these events. Proper clearing of GPIO status is needed to deassert the IFR bit. The mapping between GPIO events and CPU lines is device‑specific.
dependencies: CPU IER/ISTP configured; GPIO status clear
Remarks: Confirm event routing in the data manual before enabling IER; keep ISRs minimal to reduce latency.

description: GPIO interrupts can be used to synchronize EDMA transfers. This enables hardware‑triggered movement of data without CPU polling. The configuration binds a GPIO event line to an EDMA channel. Correct setup yields deterministic latency and throughput. Event mapping details are device‑specific and defined externally.
Remarks: Guard against bursty GPIO activity by selecting appropriate EDMA triggering mode and transfer sizes.

description: The GPIO peripheral input clock derives from the CPU clock divided by six. This relationship determines maximum toggle and sampling rates. Timing analysis should account for this division when sizing edge detection or debouncing. Software must ensure clocks are enabled before access. Clock characteristics are fixed by the silicon configuration.
Remarks: Consider external hardware debouncing when required throughput exceeds CPU/6 derived limitations.

description: GPIO clocks and resets are gated by the Power and Sleep Controller. Software must transition the module’s LPSC to ENABLE before register access. The standard PSC handshake includes programming the next state, triggering a transition, and polling for completion. Accessing registers prior to enable may return invalid data. This gating conserves power when the module is idle.
Remarks: Centralize PSC sequences in a common init to avoid duplication; always poll for transition complete before use.

description: Package pins are multiplexed among several peripheral functions including GPIO. Software selects the desired function through device‑specific mux registers. Incorrect muxing can disable GPIO visibility or drive contention. Mux configuration must precede GPIO direction and output enables. Detailed fields are provided in the data manual.
Remarks: Validate pin function in schematics and apply safe default pulls; lock mux settings early during boot.

description: The C64x+ CPU exposes IER and IFR registers to manage interrupt enables and flags. GPIO events can be routed to these CPU lines for direct servicing. Proper vector table setup via ISTP is necessary to land in the correct handler. Clearing both peripheral and CPU flags ensures forward progress. Event numbers and routes are device‑specific.
Remarks: Implement a small, fast ISR that defers work; verify vector table alignment and memory protection settings.

description: The platform includes an EDMA engine used by peripherals for high‑throughput transfers. GPIO can act as an event source to kick off EDMA sequences. Proper channel and PaRAM configuration is required for correctness. Using EDMA reduces CPU load and improves determinism. Event mapping and capabilities depend on the device configuration.
dependencies: EDMA CC/TC configured; Event router set
Remarks: Pre‑allocate channels and PaRAM sets at boot; use static mappings to keep interrupt jitter low.

description: An Inter‑Integrated Circuit controller is available for board management and peripheral control. It supports standard I2C signaling with device‑specific timing and addressing parameters. Registers and base address are defined in the user’s guide and data manual. Interrupt and DMA usage are device‑dependent. Initialization must occur after power and mux are configured.
dependencies: PSC enable; Pin multiplexing; Optional DMA/interrupts
Remarks: Use conservative bus speeds until board timing is validated; enable pull‑ups and confirm rise times.

description: A Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter provides serial communication. Configuration includes baud rate generation, framing, and FIFO behavior per the user’s guide. Register map and base address are in the device manuals. Interrupts can be used for RX/TX service. DMA support is device‑specific.
dependencies: PSC enable; Pin multiplexing; Optional DMA/interrupts
Remarks: Prefer DMA or FIFO‑driven interrupts for sustained throughput; validate clock source accuracy for baud.

description: A Serial Peripheral Interface controller supports synchronous serial links. Master/slave modes and clock polarity/phase are configurable. The register set and base address are defined in TI documentation. Interrupts and EDMA support depend on the SoC instance. Proper chip‑select handling is required for multi‑device buses.
dependencies: PSC enable; Pin multiplexing; Optional DMA/interrupts
Remarks: Use atomic GPIO or native CS timing per device requirements; verify mode (CPOL/CPHA) against datasheets.

description: The Multichannel Audio Serial Port supports complex audio streaming. It offers serializers, TDM slots, and clocking options defined in its guide. Register details and base addresses are device‑specific. High‑rate streaming typically uses DMA for efficiency. Proper pinmux and clock tree setup are prerequisites.
Remarks: Align word clocks and frame syncs precisely; validate serializer directions before enabling transmitters.

description: An external DDR2 interface provides system memory access. Controller configuration covers timing, mode registers, and PHY training per the user’s guide. Initialization must occur early in boot to enable code/data execution from DDR. Signal integrity and timing closure are board‑dependent. Base address and register map are defined in documentation.
Remarks: Perform JEDEC‑compliant init sequence; keep refresh and ODT parameters consistent with board layout.

description: A PCI peripheral enables host/device interconnect depending on configuration. It includes configuration space, BARs, and DMA features per the guide. Proper reset and clocking are required before enumeration. Interrupts and EDMA integration may be present depending on the SoC. Register details are in the device documentation.
dependencies: PSC enable; Reference clock; Reset sequencing
Remarks: Validate voltage levels and reset timing; ensure address decoding and BAR sizing match the host.

description: The UHPI provides a parallel host interface for high‑throughput access. It allows an external host to read/write internal memory and registers. Configuration is device‑specific and documented in the UHPI guide. Arbitration and bus timing must be respected. Interrupts may coordinate handshakes.
Remarks: Define a strict ownership protocol to avoid contention; buffer accesses when crossing clock domains.

description: An integrated Ethernet switch and MAC subsystem enables network connectivity. It includes MACs, an internal switch fabric, and MDIO/SGMII control as documented. Proper pinmux, clocks, and PHY connections are required. DMA is typically used for packet movement. Register sets and base addresses are defined in the user’s guide and data manual.
Remarks: Use ring‑buffered DMA for RX/TX; configure flow control and VLANs as required by the application.

feature_name: Software responsibility for clearing GPIO interrupts
description: The software must clear latched GPIO edge/status after servicing. Failure to clear can keep the request asserted and block further events. The flow involves reading status, servicing, and writing to clear registers. This pattern ensures reliable edge detection. It also prevents ISR reentry due to stale flags.
dependencies: INSTAT and edge clear registers; ISR design
Remarks: Centralize clear sequences and audit for missing clears; instrument ISRs to catch repeat firings.

description: Security‑related capabilities are not described in the provided excerpts. Any secure boot, encryption engines, or access controls must be confirmed in the device data manual. Without explicit documentation, no claims can be made about security blocks. Software should assume minimal security until verified. Consult TI literature for authoritative details.
Remarks: Engage with TI documentation to confirm presence of secure boot, key storage, or firewalls before relying on security properties.

description: Debug and trace capabilities are not detailed in the provided excerpts. Typical C64x+ devices expose JTAG and on‑chip debug, but this must be verified in manuals. Without explicit references, driver or tooling requirements cannot be asserted. Basic software debug through CPU interrupts and vector tables remains available. Consult core and device manuals for specifics.
Remarks: Treat debug as board‑ and toolchain‑dependent until confirmed; plan for non‑intrusive test points and logs.

description: A standard bring‑up involves enabling PSC/LPSC, configuring pin multiplexing, setting directions, initializing outputs atomically, configuring edges, clearing stale status, and finally enabling interrupts. This order prevents spurious events and contention. It aligns with the module’s power and clock dependencies. Each step references defined registers and flows in the manuals. The sequence should be encapsulated in reusable code.
dependencies: PSC; Pinmux; DIR01; SET/CLR; Edge enables; BINTEN
Remarks: Gate enabling of interrupts to the final step; verify mux and direction before driving any external signals.}
